title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Overview of ULIP tax benefits for NRI
The life insurance space offers a number of products that NRIs can consider. For someone who is looking for an investment avenue that also comes with the benefit of market-linked returns, ULIPs can be a worthy option. Unit-linked insurance plans can be equally lucrative for NRIs, too. FEMA, the Foreign Exchange Management Act, allows people of Indian origin and NRIs to invest in life insurance plans in India1.
ULIPs not only help you get the required coverage but also allow you to get potential returns from the market-linked funds that you invest in. One of the benefits that ULIPs can offer NRIs is the tax-saving benefit. Read on as we discuss NRI ULIP taxation implications for NRIs in detail.
Who is an NRI?
Before we delve into ULIP taxation rules for NRIs, let us understand who is an NRI. Simply put, an individual who has an Indian origin but is living in a foreign country is an NRI or Non-Resident Indian. However, residential status has to be kept in mind when determining an individual’s status. According to Section 6 of the Income Tax Act, an individual is an NRI, non-resident Indian if he/ she does not reside in India. On the other hand, an individual is considered to be a resident in India in any prior years provided:
He/ she was in India for 182 days or more in the last year, Or
He/ she was in India for 60 days or more in the last year and 365 days or more during the last 4 years immediately preceding the last year.2
Keep in mind: The Finance Act 2020 states that an Indian citizen will be considered to be a resident in India only if his/ her total income, apart from income from foreign sources, was more than INR 15 lakhs during the last year. 2
How do ULIPs help NRIs save on their tax liability?
The Income Tax Act of 1961 has a set of distinct rules for taxation when it comes to residents and NRIs. In order to know your tax implications for ULIPs for NRI, it is important to identify and understand your residential status.
As an NRI, you need to know that while the income that you earn in India is taxable, the income outside will not be taxable in India. For non-resident seafarers, their salary for service outside the country will not be taken in the taxable income, even if it comes in his/ her NRE account. Now, let us take a look at the benefits of ULIP taxation for NRI policyholders.
Switching funds does not attract any tax liability
Partial withdrawals do not attract tax
Section 80C of the Income Tax Act allows you tax benefits towards the premium that is paid towards the policy. You can claim up to INR 1.5 lakhs in a financial year against the premium that you pay towards the ULIP, depending on the following conditions:
If you purchased your ULIP on or after April 1, 2012, the premium paid should not be above 10% of the capital sum assured3
If you purchased your ULIP on or before March 1, 2012, the premium paid should not be above 20% of the capital sum assured3
The death benefit received by the nominee for ULIPs for NRI is tax-free. 3
Taxation on capital gains:
The maturity benefit you get from the ULIP can also be tax-free, making ULIPs an ideal investment for most. Keep in mind:
For ULIPs that were issued on or after February 1, 2021, Section 10(10D) allows tax deductions if the annual aggregate premium on the ULIPs (all ULIPs, if applicable) is up to ₹2.5 lakhs. 3
In case the premium is over INR 2.5 lakhs, the returns will be taxed3 as long-term capital gain tax.
How can NRIs buy ULIPs in India?
. Let us take a look at how, as an NRI, you can buy a ULIP in India:
First things first, chalk out your needs and requirements and look for a ULIP that is in line with them. Once you choose the right policy, it is all a smooth ride from there.
Your insurance company would ask you to submit a few documents to authenticate the purchase. Keep the following documents4 handy:
Passport’s canned copy as age and identity proof
Passport-sized photograph
Proof of residence - India and abroad
Income proof
People with an OCI (Overseas Citizenship of India) card or any PIO (Persons of Indian Origin) and having valid cards for the same with permanent overseas citizenship.
PAN card photocopy or a duly signed Form 60, for NRIs with Indian income.
MER or a Medical examination report, as applicable.
A mandatory Supplementary Questionnaire for Foreign Residency which is applicable for OCIs, PIOs and NRIs.
Any additional document that may be required by the consulate or the regulator as a part of their AML (Anti Money Laundering) Guidelines to fulfill their KYC (Know Your Customer) requirements of NRIs as they are deemed to be at a higher risk.
Once the paperwork is complete, your insurer will go through the application. Once all details are verified, the policy will be issued to you.
As an NRI, you have the option to pay the premiums either via an Indian bank account or via an NRE/ NRO account that is maintained with an Indian bank. You can make the payment through any of the available electronic modes.
What are other important things NRIs investing in ULIPs in India should know?
If you are an NRI who is looking for a financial umbrella that offers the benefits of insurance, investment and tax benefits, then ULIPs are indeed the answer for you. Before you make an investment, keep these things4 in mind:
ULIPs offer the flexibility to invest as per your risk appetite. You can invest in equity, debt or balanced funds.
ULIPs come with a lock-in period of 5 years.
You can invest extra funds in ULIPs through top-ups
Premiums that you pay are tax-deductible
The death benefit is tax-free
You have the option to stay invested for as long as you wish.
Why should NRIs consider investing in India?
When it comes to making an investment in India, NRIs have a lot of options that help them build a foundation back home while also offering them financial security. Over the past few years, the Indian economy has been steadily expanding. Investing in the Indian market is a great opportunity for the residents as well as NRIs.
Government reforms and redesigning have created a robust startup ecosystem, which has further pushed India to become one of the fastest-growing economies in the world. Buying financial products, managing accounts and conducting transactions have made it simpler and quite profitable for NRIs to invest in India.
How can an NRI pay the premium for investing in ULIPs?
Let us now find out more about NRI ULIP taxation. For the ULIP premium, an NRI can make the payment either through a bank account in India or through an NRE/ NRO account. One can choose any of the following options4
Internet banking
Debit cards
International/ domestic credit cards
Credit Card Standing Instructions (CCSI)
National Automated Clearing House (NACH)
Online wallets
Online ECS mandates.
Conclusion
The government allows NRIs different financial avenues in India. This not only helps the NRIs build on their savings but also gives them the opportunity for financial growth. With ULIPs, NRIs have the potential to build a corpus while securing the financial future of their loved ones. ULIPs also help in reaping tax benefits, making them a rather suitable investment option for just about everyone.
FAQs
Is ULIP tax-free for NRI?Apart from various other advantages, ULIPs act as an efficient tax-saving product for NRIs. The premium, the death benefit, and the maturity benefit allow certain tax deductions. Make sure you understand the intricacies of each tax benefit.
How are NRIs taxed on ULIP?The premiums can fetch NRIs a tax deduction of INR 1.5 lakhs a year. The maturity benefit can attract a tax if the policy was issued on or after February 1, 2021, and if the annual aggregate premium on the ULIPs (all ULIPs, if applicable) is more than INR 2.5 lakhs5.Gain from such policy is taxed as long term capital gain.
What is FATCA/CRS?FATCA stands for Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act, while CRS is Common Reporting Standard. Both are global initiatives to fight tax evasion and money laundering.
Do NRIs need to pay TDS on insurance payouts they receive?For all income generated within the geographical boundaries of India, all NRIs are needed to file their Income Tax in India. However, according to the norms of Section 195 of the Income Tax Act, TDS is applicable to all Life Insurance Policies that do not fall under the ambit of 10(10D). Hence, if the policy does not have tax-free maturity U/S 10(10D) such as Annuity Plans, then TDS would be applicable. However, if the NRI resides in a country where the favorable DTAA (Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement) condition is applicable, then certain documents such as a duly signed Form 10F along with a TRC, i.e. Tax Residency Certificate needs to be provided, before payment.
